summary: I was born in Asmara, Eritrea in 1988 and moved to Minnesota in 2011. Since arriving here, I have been a student at St. Paul College. My family and I brought with us the jebena, a traditional Eritrean coffee maker, which holds a special place in my heart because it connects me to my homeland and family traditions. Through coffee ceremonies, I keep alive the memories of my childhood and the celebrations we had back home.

Asmara is where I was born in 1988, the capital city of Eritrea. Growing up there, I experienced many family gatherings and cultural traditions, especially during holidays like Easter. It’s a place that shaped my early life and where my family and I shared many joyful moments, especially around coffee ceremonies. Even now, living in Minnesota, I often think back to Asmara and the warmth of those times.

Eritrea is my home country, located in eastern Africa bordering Sudan, Ethiopia, and Djibouti. It has nine ethnic tribes, each with unique traditions, including the use of the jebena for coffee. Growing up in Eritrea, coffee ceremonies were an important part of family life, especially on Sundays and holidays like Easter. Though I moved to Minnesota in 2011, Eritrea’s culture and memories remain close to my heart.

I moved to Minnesota in 2011 with my family, bringing with us our traditions and the jebena coffee pot from Eritrea. Here in Minnesota, we continue to gather on Sundays after church and on holidays to share coffee and create new memories. Though it’s far from home, Minnesota has become a place where we can blend our Eritrean heritage with our new life in America.

Since moving to Minnesota, I have been a student at St. Paul College. Studying there has been an important part of my life as I adapt to my new environment. While I focus on my education, I also cherish my Eritrean roots and the traditions I brought with me, like the coffee ceremonies we continue to hold with my family.

Sudan is one of the countries that borders Eritrea, my homeland. It is part of the region where the coffee culture with the jebena is shared and appreciated. Knowing that Sudan is close to Eritrea helps me understand the cultural connections and similarities in traditions across these neighboring countries.

Ethiopia borders Eritrea and shares some cultural traditions with us, including the use of the jebena for coffee. Growing up, I was aware of these neighboring countries and their influence on our customs. The proximity of Ethiopia to Eritrea reminds me of the shared heritage and the regional importance of coffee ceremonies.

Djibouti is another country that borders Eritrea, my home country. Like Eritrea, Djibouti is part of the region where the jebena coffee tradition is common. Knowing about Djibouti helps me appreciate the cultural landscape of the Horn of Africa and the connections between our peoples.

Easter was always a special time for my family back in Eritrea. We would all gather at my house and celebrate together for three days, sharing food and coffee made by my mom using the jebena. These celebrations created lasting memories of family, faith, and tradition that I carry with me even now in Minnesota.
